children.name: Cricetus
children.description: The common hamster — once one of the most abundant mammals of the European steppe and farmland, now critically endangered across western Europe. It is a solitary, aggressive rodent that hoards enormous quantities of seeds and roots in its burrow system — up to 65 kg have been found in a single cache. Common hamsters hibernate individually and have been recorded carrying grain in their pouches by running back to their burrows hundreds of times in a single day. Intensive agriculture, pesticide use, and the conversion of grain crops to maize (which provides poor nutrition) have caused catastrophic population collapses.

children.name: Cricetus cricetus
children.description: Critically endangered in France, Belgium, and the Netherlands. Larger than pet hamsters with striking black, white, and chestnut patterning. Will aggressively rear up and hiss at threats much larger than itself.

children.name: Cricetulus
children.description: Rat-like and mouse-like dwarf hamsters of Central and East Asia — small, long-tailed hamsters occupying steppes, semi-deserts, and scrublands from eastern Europe to China. Unlike the true hamsters, Cricetulus species have longer tails relative to body size and are more omnivorous. Several species are common in the exotic pet trade. They are solitary and strongly territorial, with scent-gland marking playing a central role in individual spacing.

children.name: Cricetulus barabensis
children.description: Widely distributed across the steppes of Russia, Kazakhstan, Mongolia, and northern China. Named for the Baraba steppe of western Siberia. Bears a distinctive dark dorsal stripe and is an important agricultural pest on cereal crops.

children.name: Cricetulus griseus
children.description: Native to the deserts and grasslands of northern China and Mongolia. Achieved worldwide scientific fame as the source of Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO) cells, the dominant cell line used in biopharmaceutical manufacturing. In the wild it is nocturnal and largely insectivorous.

children.name: Cricetulus migratorius
children.description: The most widespread Cricetulus, ranging from Greece east to Pakistan. Inhabits rocky terrain and agricultural fields. Despite the specific epithet, it does not truly migrate but undergoes local dispersal movements in autumn.

children.name: Cricetulus longicaudatus
children.description: Inhabits rocky montane slopes of central Asia, Mongolia, and northern China. Has a noticeably longer tail than other Cricetulus species. Feeds on seeds, roots, and invertebrates and caches food in its burrow for winter.

children.name: Cricetulus sokolovi
children.description: Named after Vladimir Sokolov; from Mongolia and Inner Mongolia with complex burrow systems.

children.name: Cricetulus kamensis
children.description: A Tibetan plateau species above 3500 m; densest fur of any Cricetulus; hibernates 6 months.

children.name: Cricetulus albida
children.description: A pale sand-coloured species from the Gobi Desert; camouflaged against gypsum salt flats.

children.name: Mesocricetus
children.description: Golden hamsters — small solitary rodents native to the dry scrubland of Syria and Turkey. All golden hamsters in captivity worldwide descend from a single wild female and her pups captured near Aleppo, Syria in 1930. In the wild, golden hamsters are nocturnal, solitary, and highly aggressive towards conspecifics outside the breeding season. They were introduced to western laboratories as research animals in the 1930s and became popular pets in the 1940s. Wild populations are now threatened by habitat loss and are considered vulnerable.

children.name: Mesocricetus auratus
children.description: The familiar pet hamster. Wild populations survive in a small area of northwestern Syria and are listed as vulnerable. Their cheek pouches extend back along the sides of their body to their hips.

children.name: Mesocricetus brandti
children.description: Found in Turkey, the Caucasus, and northern Iran. Larger and darker than the golden hamster. Named after the German naturalist Johann Friedrich von Brandt. Forms small colonies unlike the strictly solitary golden hamster.

children.name: Mesocricetus newtoni
children.description: Restricted to Romania and Bulgaria, inhabiting grassland and agricultural steppes. Named after the ornithologist Alfred Newton. A near-threatened species due to agricultural intensification and habitat fragmentation in its restricted range.

children.name: Mesocricetus newtoni
children.description: A threatened species endemic to Romania and Bulgaria; declined due to intensive farming.

children.name: Mesocricetus raddei
children.description: From northern Caucasus and southern Russia; occupies a transitional steppe zone.

children.name: Phodopus
children.description: Dwarf hamsters of the genus Phodopus — tiny, densely furred hamsters of Central Asian deserts and steppes. They are the smallest hamsters, with adults weighing just 15–40 g. Unlike most hamsters they do not hibernate; instead they enter daily torpor during cold spells and cache food in their burrow. The Djungarian hamster is one of the few hamsters in which males play a direct role in raising the young, assisting with birth and pup care.

children.name: Phodopus sungorus
children.description: Native to the steppes of Kazakhstan and southwest Siberia. In winter the grey-brown coat turns almost entirely white, providing camouflage in snow. One of the most popular pet hamsters in Europe and Asia. Males assist in birth, pulling pups from the birth canal.

children.name: Phodopus roborovskii
children.description: The smallest and fastest of all domesticated hamsters. Native to the deserts of China, Mongolia, and Kazakhstan. Named after the Russian explorer Vsevolod Roborovski who collected specimens in 1894. Can run up to 9 km per night on a wheel.

children.name: Phodopus campbelli
children.description: Popular pet from the steppes of Tuva and Mongolia; exhibits social monogamy.

children.name: Phodopus sundorus
children.description: The smallest Phodopus from the Dzungarian Basin; hibernates in winter.

children.name: Phodopus przewalskii
children.description: Known from only a few Qaidam Basin localities; one of the least-studied Palearctic hamsters.

children.name: Microtus
children.description: Voles — short-tailed, small-eyed grassland and woodland rodents that are among the most abundant mammals on Earth. Vole populations undergo dramatic cycles, typically peaking every 3–5 years then crashing. These cycles drive corresponding cycles in owl, hawk, and fox populations across the Northern Hemisphere. The common vole can produce up to 7 litters of 4–8 young per year, reaching sexual maturity at just 2–3 weeks of age. They create dense runway systems through grass and are critical to the ecology of virtually every northern terrestrial ecosystem.

children.name: Microtus arvalis
children.description: The most important prey item for barn owls, kestrels, and rough-legged buzzards across Europe. Population explosions can strip cereal crops. Capable of producing 15–18 young in a single month.

children.name: Microtus agrestis
children.description: Britain's most abundant small mammal. Prefers longer, rougher grass than the common vole. A barn owl family needs roughly 1,000 field voles per year to successfully raise a brood.

children.name: Microtus pennsylvanicus
children.description: The most widespread vole in North America, ranging from Alaska to northern Mexico. Forms conspicuous runways through grass. A critical prey species for virtually every North American raptor and mustelid. Undergoes 3–5 year population cycles.

children.name: Microtus californicus
children.description: Abundant in grasslands of California and Baja California. Populations can reach extraordinary densities during peak years, causing significant damage to orchards and vineyards. One of the most studied rodents in population ecology research.

children.name: Microtus ochrogaster
children.description: Famous in neuroscience research for its monogamous pair-bonding, driven by oxytocin and vasopressin receptor distribution in the brain. Extensively studied to understand the neurobiology of social attachment and love. Inhabits tallgrass prairies of central North America.

children.name: Microtus pinetorum
children.description: A highly fossorial vole of eastern North American forests, spending most of its life in underground tunnel systems. Has short, dense fur adapted to a burrowing lifestyle. Forages primarily on roots, tubers, and subterranean fungi.

children.name: Microtus montanus
children.description: Inhabits montane meadows and sagebrush flats across the Rocky Mountains and Great Basin. Highly productive species able to generate large population explosions. Contrasts with the prairie vole in being promiscuous rather than monogamous.

children.name: Microtus oeconomus
children.description: One of the most widespread voles in the world, circumpolar in distribution across Arctic and subarctic tundra. Tolerates wet habitats including tundra bogs. A key prey species supporting Arctic fox, snowy owl, and short-eared owl across its range.

children.name: Microtus subterraneus
children.description: A burrowing vole of central and southern European forests and orchards. Has reduced eyes and dense, velvety fur adapted to underground life. Causes considerable economic damage by gnawing on tree roots in orchards, and is a significant pest in viticulture.

children.name: Microtus richardsoni
children.description: North America's largest vole, closely associated with streams and wet meadows in the Rocky Mountains. Named after the Arctic explorer Sir John Richardson. Semi-aquatic in habits and a strong swimmer. Feeds extensively on aquatic and riparian vegetation.

children.name: Microtus agrestis
children.description: A grass-eating vole exhibiting 3-4 year population cycles in northern latitudes.

children.name: Microtus arvalis
children.description: One of the most abundant European mammals; peak densities exceed 2000 per hectare.

children.name: Microtus ochrogaster
children.description: A monogamous vole; model organism for neurobiology research on pair-bonding and oxytocin.

children.name: Microtus pennsylvanicus
children.description: The most widely distributed vole in North America; creates surface runways in grass.

children.name: Myodes
children.description: Bank voles — woodland-adapted voles with a redder coat and more prominent ears than field voles. The bank vole is one of the most widespread small mammals in European woodland and is particularly associated with dense, shrubby understorey. It is an important vector of hantavirus in Europe. Unlike field voles, bank voles are good climbers and will ascend into shrubs and low branches to eat berries and catkins. They are often the only small mammal present in dense conifer plantations.

children.name: Myodes glareolus
children.description: Distinguished from other voles by its chestnut back. A key prey species for tawny owls, weasels, and stoats in woodland. Puumala hantavirus is carried asymptomatically by bank voles in Scandinavia and causes nephropathia epidemica in humans.

children.name: Myodes rufocanus
children.description: Inhabits boreal and montane forests from Scandinavia east to Japan and Hokkaido. Distinguished by grey flanks contrasting with a rufous back. Participates in the famous 3–5 year vole cycles that regulate predator populations across boreal Eurasia.

children.name: Myodes rutilus
children.description: Circumpolar in taiga and tundra-edge habitats, ranging from Scandinavia across Siberia to Alaska and northern Canada. Bright chestnut dorsal stripe distinguishes it from other red-backed voles. An important prey base for short-eared owls and Arctic foxes.

children.name: Myodes gapperi
children.description: Abundant in coniferous and mixed forests across northern North America. Named after William Gapper, a British natural historian. Particularly associated with woody debris and mossy logs on the forest floor. An important prey species for the northern spotted owl.

children.name: Myodes rufocanus
children.description: A boreal vole with reddish sides; key prey for owls and foxes across taiga ecosystems.

children.name: Myodes shantaricus
children.description: Endemic to the Shantar Islands; isolated since the last glacial period.

children.name: Arvicola
children.description: Water voles — the largest voles in the Palearctic, inhabiting riverbanks, wetlands, and, in some populations, dry farmland. The European water vole, made famous by 'Ratty' in The Wind in the Willows, has suffered catastrophic declines across the UK due to American mink predation. Arvicola voles are excellent swimmers and divers, and their bankside burrow systems create characteristic spoil heaps. Fossorial populations in the Alps cause severe damage to orchards and hayfields by gnawing root systems.

children.name: Arvicola amphibius
children.description: Britain's fastest declining mammal, losing over 90% of its range since the 1970s due to American mink predation. The inspiration for 'Ratty' in Kenneth Grahame's Wind in the Willows. Subject to major reintroduction programmes across England.

children.name: Arvicola scherman
children.description: A fossorial form found in Alpine meadows and orchards from France to the Balkans. Unlike the semi-aquatic A. amphibius, this species rarely ventures to water, instead living in deep subterranean tunnel systems. A major agricultural pest in central European orchards and hayfields.

children.name: Lemmus
children.description: True lemmings of the Arctic tundra — stout, furry rodents famous for dramatic population cycles. Every 3–5 years, lemming populations explode then crash, driving corresponding crashes in snowy owl, Arctic fox, and rough-legged buzzard populations. The myth of mass suicide — lemmings throwing themselves off cliffs — was popularised by a staged Disney documentary in 1958; in reality, lemmings migrate in search of food and some drown crossing rivers, but there is no suicidal behaviour. Lemmings are active year-round under the snow, where insulated subnivean runways maintain temperatures above freezing.

children.name: Lemmus lemmus
children.description: The most colourful rodent of northern Europe — boldly patterned in black, rust, and white. The national animal of Norwegian folklore. Snowy owl breeding success is almost entirely dependent on lemming population peaks.

children.name: Lemmus sibiricus
children.description: The most widespread Lemmus, found across the entire Siberian Arctic tundra east to Alaska. Brown in summer and paler in winter, though does not turn fully white. Undergoes the same dramatic population cycles as the Norway lemming with consequences for the entire Arctic food web.

children.name: Lemmus trimucronatus
children.description: Found across Arctic tundra of Alaska and northern Canada. Feeds primarily on grasses, sedges, and mosses. During population peaks, mass migrations occur with animals swimming across rivers and fjords. Population cycles synchronised with those of the snowy owl and rough-legged hawk.

children.name: Lemmus sibiricus
children.description: A true lemming with circumpolar distribution; primary prey for Arctic foxes and snowy owls.

children.name: Dicrostonyx
children.description: Collared lemmings — the only rodents that turn completely white in winter. Dicrostonyx species develop enlarged, bifurcated claws in winter for digging through hard-packed snow. They are circumpolar in distribution and occupy the driest and coldest Arctic habitats, preferring rocky fell-fields and polar deserts where true lemmings are rare. Like other lemmings they do not hibernate and remain active through the Arctic winter in subnivean galleries, feeding on cached vegetation.

children.name: Dicrostonyx torquatus
children.description: Inhabits Arctic tundra from Norway to the Yenisei River in Siberia. The 'collar' refers to a pale band running from the cheeks to the back of the neck. One of the key prey items of the snowy owl in Eurasian Arctic. Its claws enlarge dramatically in winter to function as ice-picks.

children.name: Dicrostonyx groenlandicus
children.description: Found across Arctic Canada and Greenland. The only terrestrial mammal native to Greenland apart from the Arctic fox and polar bear. Populations on the high Arctic islands experience extreme winter conditions with no plant material available above snow; relies entirely on cached food stores.

children.name: Dicrostonyx richardsoni
children.description: Restricted to the tundra of northern Manitoba and Nunavut. Named after the Arctic naturalist Sir John Richardson. Isolated population on Southampton Island in Hudson Bay with a distinctive pelage. Prey for rough-legged hawks, snowy owls, and Arctic foxes.

children.name: Dicrostonyx groenlandicus
children.description: Grows white winter coat and enlarged curved claws for digging through packed snow.

children.name: Ondatra
children.description: The muskrat — a large, semi-aquatic vole native to North America, now established across much of Europe and Asia following escapes from fur farms in the early 20th century. Despite its name and superficial appearance, the muskrat is not a true rat but a highly modified vole. It builds lodge-like structures from vegetation in marshes and ponds, and its burrows can undermine dykes and riverbanks. It is one of the most important fur-bearing animals in North America; tens of millions were trapped annually in the mid-20th century.

children.name: Ondatra zibethicus
children.description: A significant invasive species in Europe, where it is controlled to protect agricultural drainage infrastructure. Swims using a laterally flattened, scaly tail. Can stay submerged for up to 17 minutes.

children.name: Peromyscus
children.description: Deer mice and white-footed mice — the dominant small rodents of North and Central America, with nearly 60 species occupying habitats from Arctic tundra to tropical cloud forest. Peromyscus are distinguished by their large eyes, large ears, bicoloured tails, and white underparts. They are the principal reservoir of Sin Nombre hantavirus, which causes hantavirus pulmonary syndrome with high mortality in humans. Deer mice were among the first mammals to recolonise Mount St. Helens following the 1980 eruption.

children.name: Peromyscus maniculatus
children.description: The most widespread North American rodent, found from the Yukon to central Mexico. The primary reservoir of Sin Nombre hantavirus. Highly variable ecologically — populations in open grassland are short-tailed and grey while forest populations are long-tailed and dark brown.

children.name: Peromyscus leucopus
children.description: Abundant in eastern North American forests and woodlots. The primary reservoir host of Borrelia burgdorferi, the bacterium causing Lyme disease. Population explosions in mast years (high acorn production) drive subsequent epidemics of Lyme disease.

children.name: Peromyscus californicus
children.description: The largest Peromyscus, restricted to chaparral and woodland in California and Baja California. Monogamous with strong pair bonds; males invest heavily in pup care. Studied as a comparison species to prairie voles in research on the neurobiology of monogamy.

children.name: Peromyscus polionotus
children.description: Inhabitant of old fields and coastal dunes of the southeastern United States. Pale beach-adapted subspecies on Florida's Gulf Coast barrier islands are a focus of evolutionary research on rapid cryptic coloration. Strictly monogamous with extensive biparental care.

children.name: Peromyscus gossypinus
children.description: A large, dark Peromyscus of the southeastern United States, particularly associated with bottomland hardwood forests and swamps. An excellent climber, readily ascending into the canopy to feed on tree seeds and fungi. Named for its cotton-field habitat association.

children.name: Peromyscus truei
children.description: Closely associated with pinyon-juniper woodland across the Great Basin and Southwest. Has distinctly oversized ears for a Peromyscus. Named after Frederick True of the Smithsonian Institution. Heavily dependent on pinyon pine seeds and particularly vulnerable to drought-driven pinyon die-offs.

children.name: Peromyscus maniculatus
children.description: The most widespread North American mammal; first Peromyscus to have its genome sequenced.

children.name: Peromyscus leucopus
children.description: A common woodland mouse; primary reservoir for Lyme disease in the eastern US.

children.name: Peromyscus californicus
children.description: A larger species from coastal chaparral; one of few monogamous Peromyscus.

children.name: Peromyscus polionotus
children.description: A small pale mouse of the southeastern US; constructs elaborate burrows with escape tunnels.

children.name: Reithrodontomys
children.description: Harvest mice of the New World — tiny, grooved-toothed mice that build spherical nests suspended in grass or shrubs. With some species weighing less than 5 g, they are among the smallest North American rodents. The grooved upper incisors that give the genus its name distinguish them from all other North American mice. Most species inhabit grasslands, marshes, or forest edges. Salt marsh harvest mice are among the most endangered rodents in North America, restricted to tidal marshes of San Francisco Bay.

children.name: Reithrodontomys megalotis
children.description: The most widespread harvest mouse in North America, from British Columbia to southern Mexico. Builds a softball-sized nest of shredded grass suspended in vegetation. Despite weighing only 10 g, maintains territories and is intolerant of conspecifics.

children.name: Reithrodontomys humilis
children.description: The only harvest mouse in eastern North America, inhabiting old fields, grasslands, and forest edges from the Midwest to the Atlantic. Often overlooked due to its tiny size and cryptic habits. Nests are so well concealed that the species was considered rare until systematic trapping.

children.name: Reithrodontomys raviventris
children.description: An endangered species restricted entirely to tidal salt marshes of San Francisco Bay. Can tolerate salt water, a unique ability among North American mice. Threatened by sea-level rise and development. Extensive habitat restoration efforts are ongoing around the Bay.

children.name: Reithrodontomys megalotis
children.description: A small mouse with grooved upper incisor; builds spherical grass nests above ground.

children.name: Reithrodontomys humulis
children.description: The smallest harvest mouse with prehensile tail for climbing grass stems.

children.name: Sigmodon
children.description: Cotton rats — large, shaggy-furred rats of open grasslands from the United States to South America. The hispid cotton rat is one of the most important small mammal pests in the southeastern United States and is also the primary reservoir of several arenaviruses. Cotton rats are among the few wild rodents that can be infected with human influenza viruses, making them a model organism for influenza research. They create dense runways through grass and produce multiple large litters per year.

children.name: Sigmodon hispidus
children.description: The most common cotton rat in the United States, abundant in grasslands and fields from Virginia to Central America. A reservoir of Black Creek Canal hantavirus and several arenaviruses. Used extensively in biomedical research on respiratory viruses including influenza and RSV.

children.name: Sigmodon fulviventer
children.description: Inhabits semi-arid grasslands and shrublands from Arizona and New Mexico south to central Mexico. Distinguished by its tawny underparts. Less abundant than the hispid cotton rat and prefers drier, more open habitats with sparse grass cover.

children.name: Sigmodon ochrognathus
children.description: Restricted to montane grasslands and pine-oak forests of the Chihuahuan Desert region of Texas and Mexico at elevations above 1,500 m. Has a distinctive yellowish nose and facial markings. The most range-restricted of the North American cotton rats.

children.name: Sigmodon hispidus
children.description: A robust coarse-furred rat of grasslands; major pest of sugarcane and cotton.

children.name: Sigmodon arizonae
children.description: A southwestern species found in desert riparian corridors.

children.name: Neotoma
children.description: Woodrats or packrats — large-eared, bushy-tailed rats famous for constructing middens — accumulations of sticks, bones, dung, and debris that may persist for thousands of years in dry caves. Packrat middens are invaluable palaeoclimatic archives, preserving plant remains and pollen that document past environments going back 40,000 years. Woodrats are the only rodents that eat the toxic foliage of creosote bush, sequestering the resins harmlessly. They are colonial in some species and highly territorial in others.

children.name: Neotoma floridana
children.description: Found in rocky forests and thickets of the eastern United States. Builds large stick houses at the base of trees or rock outcrops. Several island subspecies, including the Key Largo woodrat, are federally endangered. Feeds on berries, nuts, fungi, and green vegetation.

children.name: Neotoma albigula
children.description: The most widespread woodrat in the Chihuahuan Desert, ranging from Utah to central Mexico. Builds large cactus-defended middens in desert scrub. Specialised for eating cactus — a diet toxic to other rodents — by harbouring detoxifying gut bacteria.

children.name: Neotoma lepida
children.description: Inhabits the Mojave and Great Basin deserts. Its ancient middens, preserved in dry caves, contain plant fossils that document desert expansion since the last glacial maximum. One of the palaeoclimatic study animals, providing 40,000-year environmental records.

children.name: Neotoma cinerea
children.description: The only woodrat with a squirrel-like bushy tail. Ranges from the Yukon to New Mexico in rocky montane habitats. Has a strong, musky odour produced by flank glands. Builds middens in cliff crevices that can accumulate for millennia.

children.name: Neotoma cinerea
children.description: The most widespread Neotoma; builds prominent stick middens providing shelter for dozens of species.

children.name: Neotoma lepida
children.description: A small pale woodrat; urine crystallises around its nest preserving plant fragments for millennia.

children.name: Synaptomys
children.description: Bog lemmings of North America — superficially vole-like but more closely related to true lemmings, occupying sphagnum bogs, wet meadows, and boreal forest edge habitats. Distinguished from voles by their short tails, grooved upper incisors, and preference for wet habitats. The northern bog lemming is the only North American rodent whose range is essentially restricted to boreal and subarctic zones. Both species are considered indicators of sphagnum bog integrity.

children.name: Synaptomys cooperi
children.description: Found in wet meadows and bogs from Manitoba to North Carolina. Named after William Cooper, a co-founder of the New York Lyceum of Natural History. Creates extensive runways through sphagnum moss. Can swim well and is not restricted to bogs — also uses upland grass habitats.

children.name: Synaptomys borealis
children.description: Restricted to boreal forest and tundra from Alaska to Labrador. The rarest and least-known North American rodent — rarely trapped and almost never observed. Associated with sphagnum bogs and wet sedge meadows in coniferous forests. Feeds primarily on mosses and sedges.

children.name: Phenacomys
children.description: Heather voles of North America — small, plump voles of boreal and montane habitats, closely associated with heather-dominated vegetation. The genus contains just two species, both restricted to North America. Heather voles build elaborate grass nests under snow in winter and are active year-round. They are poorly known due to the difficulty of trapping them in their dense montane habitats. The mountain heather vole is associated with subalpine fir forest and is rarely encountered away from high-elevation meadows.

children.name: Phenacomys intermedius
children.description: Found in boreal forests and alpine meadows from Yukon to New Mexico. Closely associated with heather (Cassiope) and bilberry, which it clips into small pieces before caching near its nest. One of the few herbivores specialised on heathland vegetation.

children.name: Phenacomys longicaudus
children.description: A highly arboreal vole restricted to old-growth Douglas fir forests of coastal Oregon and northern California. Spends almost its entire life in the forest canopy, feeding exclusively on Douglas fir needles — one of the most specialised diets of any rodent. A prey species of the northern spotted owl.

children.name: Phenacomys ungava
children.description: Restricted to boreal forest and shrub tundra of eastern Canada from Manitoba to Nova Scotia. Rarely encountered; known from few museum specimens. Feeds on grasses, sedges, and dwarf shrubs. Sometimes treated as a subspecies of P. intermedius.

children.name: Ellobius
children.description: Mole voles of Central Asia — highly fossorial rodents with reduced eyes, shovel-like forefeet, and protruding incisors used for cutting through roots. They are among the most underground-adapted rodents in the Old World, rivalling true moles in their subterranean lifestyle. Several Ellobius species have remarkable sex determination — some populations lack a Y chromosome entirely and have no SRY gene, making them a focus of research into alternative mammalian sex-determining mechanisms.

children.name: Ellobius talpinus
children.description: Found in steppe and semi-desert from Ukraine east to Kazakhstan. Lives almost entirely underground, using incisors and forefeet to excavate burrows. Has reduced, partially hidden ears and tiny eyes. Feeds entirely on underground roots, bulbs, and tubers.

children.name: Ellobius fuscocapillus
children.description: Inhabits stony and loamy soils of Afghanistan, Pakistan, and Iran. Has a remarkable sex determination system — both sexes have an XX genotype with no functional Y chromosome, representing an evolutionary loss of canonical mammalian sex determination.

children.name: Ellobius talpinus
children.description: A blind mole-like vole with reduced eyes; spends most of its life underground.

children.name: Ellobius lutescens
children.description: Has lost the Y chromosome entirely — males are XO; one of few mammals without a Y chromosome.

children.name: Lagurus
children.description: Steppe lemmings — short-tailed, robust voles of Eurasian and North American steppe and prairie. Despite the common name they are not true lemmings but are related to voles. The steppe lemming of Central Asia undergoes dramatic population eruptions that can devastate cereal crops across Kazakhstan and the Russian steppe. The sagebrush vole of North America is the only North American representative and is closely associated with sagebrush-dominated habitats in the Great Basin.

children.name: Lagurus lagurus
children.description: Inhabits arid steppes from Ukraine east to Mongolia. Periodically erupts to huge population densities that destroy grain crops across the Kazakh steppe. Unlike true lemmings its population cycles are irregular and climate-dependent rather than clock-like.

children.name: Lagurus lagurus
children.description: A small grey vole of the Eurasian steppe; exhibits dramatic population cycles.

children.name: Alticola
children.description: High-elevation voles of Central Asian talus slopes; longer softer fur than Microtus.

children.name: Alticola roylei
children.description: A Himalayan vole at 2500-4000 m in India, Nepal and Pakistan.

children.name: Alticola argentatus
children.description: Widespread from Tien Shan to Altai; silvery-grey pelage on lichen-covered granite.

children.name: Alticola stoliczkanus
children.description: From the Pamir and western Himalayan ranges.

children.name: Alticola barakshin
children.description: A Mongolian species with reddish-brown dorsal stripe.
